title: Case on Crimes of Publishing False Information and of Defaming Candidates under Public Official Election Act
text:
- 204 -Case on Crimes of Publishing False Information and of Defaming 
Candidates under Public Official Election Act
[2023Hun-Ba78, June 27, 2024]
In this case, the Court held that Article 250, Section (2) of the Public 
Official Election Act, which punishes an individual publishing false 
information about a prospective candidate, does not violate the 
Constitution. However, it held unconstitutional Article 251 of the same 
Act, which punishes an individual who defames a prospective candidate, 
reasoning that this clause violates the rule against excessive restriction 
and freedom of political expression. 
Background of the Case
Complainant ran as a candidate for district mayor in the 7th 
Nationwide Simultaneous Local Elections held on June 13, 2018, but 
was unsuccessful. 
Complainant was prosecuted on charges of, inter alia, violating the 
Public Official Election Act for “publishing false information and 
defaming prospective candidates with the intent of preventing them from 
being elected as a candidate for district mayor in the 7th Nationwide 
Simultaneous Local Elections on June 13, 2018, or as a candidate in the 
by-election for a constituency in the National Assembly held on the 
same day,” on April 22, 2018 and May 6, 2018, respectively. On 
September 27, 2019, Complainant was sentenced to a fine of 6 million 
won in the trial court. After going through both the appellate court and 
the Supreme Court, the sentence of a 6 million won fine was finalized. 
During the Supreme Court proceedings, Complainant petitioned the 
court to request constitutional review of both Article 250, Section (2) 
and Article 251 of the Public Official Election Act. After that petition 
was rejected, Complainant filed a constitutional complaint.
- 205 -Subject Matter of Review
The subject matter of review in this case is whether the following 
violate the Constitution: 1) the portion of Article 250, Section (2) of the 
Public Official Election Act (amended by Act No. 5262 on January 13, 
1997) regarding an individual publishing false information about a 
person who intends to be a candidate (hereinafter referred to as the 
“Publication Prohibition Clause”); and 2) the portion of Article 251 of 
the Public Official Election Act (enacted by Act No. 4739 on March 16, 
1994, and hereinafter referred to as the “Public Official Election Act” 
regardless of amendments) concerning a person who intends to be a 
candidate (hereinafter referred to as the “Defamation Prohibition Clause,” 
and collectively with the Publication Prohibition Clause as the “Clauses 
at Issue”). The Clauses at Issue read as follows. 
Clauses at Issue
Public Official Election Act (amended by Act No. 5262 on January 
13, 1997)
Article 250 (Crime of Publishing False Information)
(2) Any person who publishes, or makes another person publish, any 
false facts on a candidate, his or her spouse, lineal ascendants or 
descendants, or siblings, so as to be unfavorable to the candidate through 
a speech, broadcast, newspaper, communication, magazine, poster, 
propaganda document, or other means, with the intention of stopping the 
candidate from being elected, or persons who possess a propaganda 
document in which a false fact is entered with the intention of 
distributing it, shall be punished by imprisonment with labor for not 
more than seven years or by a fine of not less than five million won nor 
more than 30 million won.
- 206 -Public Official Election Act (enacted by Act No. 4739 on March 16, 
1994)
Article 251 (Crime of Defaming a Candidate)
Any person who defames a candidate (including a person who intends 
to be a candidate), his or her spouse, lineal ascendants or descendants, 
siblings by pointing out any fact openly through a speech, broadcast, 
newspaper, communication, magazine, poster, propaganda document, or 
other means, with the intention of getting elected, or getting another 
person to be or not to be elected, shall be punished by imprisonment 
with labor for not more than three years or by a fine not exceeding five 
million won: Provided , That where it is a true fact and concerns a public 
interest, he or she shall not be punished.
Summary of the Decision
1. Assessment of the Publication Prohibition Clause
The term “false facts” in the Publication Prohibition Clause refers to 
facts that do not align with objective truth and therefore does not violate 
the void-for-vagueness doctrine under the nulla poena sine lege 
principle. Additionally, this clause, which aims to ensure the fairness of 
elections, prohibits limited types of conduct, and it is difficult to propose 
alternative measures. Thus, it cannot be considered to infringe freedom 
of political expression.
2. Assessment of the Defamation Prohibition Clause
A. Whether it violates the void-for-vagueness doctrine
The term “defamation” under the Defamation Prohibition Clause is 
interpreted as “undermining or disparaging others without just cause 
- 207 -regarding matters that are respected in social life.” Since the meaning 
of this term is not ambiguous, it does not violate the void-for-vagueness 
doctrine under the nulla poena sine lege principle.
B. Whether it violates freedom of political expression
The Defamation Prohibition Clause is intended to protect the dignity 
and honor of those seeking to become candidates and to ensure the 
fairness of elections. Defamatory actions involving false facts can be 
sufficiently punished under the Publication Prohibition Clause. In cases 
where the facts are not false, it is up to the prospective candidate to 
refute them, allowing voters to obtain accurate information to assess the 
candidate’s abilities, qualifications, etc. Regulating such behavior under 
the Public Official Election Act imposes an excessive restriction on 
freedom of political expression. The proviso of the Defamation 
Prohibition Clause provides a justification defense that exempts 
punishment when the act involves truthful facts concerning the public 
interest. Nevertheless, a person whose behavior meets the elements of 
the offense may still be at risk of investigation or criminal prosecution. 
Due to the uncertainty of the standards by which the public interest will 
be proven and assessed, this may have a chilling effect on freedom of 
expression. Furthermore, even without the Defamation Prohibition 
Clause, factual defamation can be punished under Article 307, Section 
(1) of the Criminal Act, ensuring that it remains punishable. Therefore, it 
cannot be concluded that negative campaigning would necessarily 
increase, and investigative and judicial authorities can still conduct 
investigations and trials swiftly, considering the factual defamation’s 
relevance to the election.
Moreover, election fairness includes the selection of candidates based 
on the disclosure of information relevant to their suitability for public 
office. Overly restricting factual statements about those seeking candidacy 
could, in fact, undermine the fairness of elections. Prospective candidates 
- 208 -voluntarily enter the public arena and should, therefore, be prepared to 
endure a certain level of negative expression about themselves.
Accordingly, the Defamation Prohibition Clause violates the rule 
against excessive restriction and infringes freedom of political expression.
Summary of Dissenting Opinion of Three Justices
In its 2011Hun-Ba75 ruling on June 27, 2013, the Court determined 
that the Defamation Prohibition Clause does not infringe freedom of 
election campaigning or political expression. Given the current reality 
where much political expression occurs through information and 
communications networks, information posted online can be indefinitely 
stored, reproduced, and disseminated. This, combined with negative 
phenomena in the internet environment, such as biased information 
acquisition, poses a significant risk to the fairness of elections. Further, 
if the act in question were punished solely under Article 307, Section (1) 
of the Criminal Act for defamation by factual assertion, the special 
provisions under the Public Official Election Act would not apply, 
potentially leading to delays in investigations and trials. This could 
encourage negative campaigning, as candidates would not face the 
disadvantages imposed by the Public Official Election Act, thereby 
increasing the risk of a chaotic election process. Considering these 
factors, there is no change in circumstances or necessity that would 
justify departing from the precedent in this case. Therefore, the 
Defamation Prohibition Clause does not infringe freedom of political 
expression.
